It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 49 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I've been exploring for 49 actions without completing the level. I need to fundamentally rethink my approach. Let me look at the history more carefully to understand what the initial state was and what changes I've made.
Let me check the very first frame to understand the initial configuration.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check the initial frame to understand starting state
init_seg = history[0].frame.segmentation
init_nodes = init_seg['nodes']
print("Initial frame nodes:")
for n in init_nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['S', 'Y', 'O', 'B']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}...")
# Also check initial ascii for bars
init_ascii = history[0].frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Initial top W:", init_ascii[0].count('W'), "Initial bottom W:", init_ascii[63].count('W'))
print("Initial step:", history[0].frame.step)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the initial state clearly:
- Initial left square: rows 49-53, cols 19-23
- Initial right square: rows 49-53, cols 39-43
Both squares started at the SAME row (49-53)! They were at the bottom of the maze.
So the initial configuration already has both squares at the same row. The goal can't be to get them to the same row since they started there.
Let me reconsider. The initial positions are:
- Left: rows 49-53, cols 19-23
- Right: rows 49-53, cols 39-43
The gap between them is cols 24-38 (15 cells).
What if the goal is to bring the squares together? Let me try pressing RIGHT to move them towards the center.
From initial:
- LEFT square at cols 19-23: RIGHT → 24-28
- RIGHT square at cols 39-43: RIGHT → 34-38
After one RIGHT, the gap is cols 29-33 (5 cells).
After two RIGHTs:
- LEFT square at cols 24-28: RIGHT → 29-33
- RIGHT square at cols 34-38: RIGHT → 29-33
They'd overlap at cols 29-33! But wait, at rows 49-53, cols 29-33 are YYYOO (colored), not black. So the squares can't move there.
Actually, let me check: at rows 49-53, what's at cols 24-38?
From the ascii I printed earlier:
Row 49: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
Cols 10-26: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB (17 B's)
Cols 27-29: YYY
Cols 30-31: OO
Cols 32-49: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
So at rows 49-53, cols 24-26 are B (black) and cols 27-29 are Y (yellow) and cols 30-31 are O (orange) and cols 32-38 are B (black).
Wait, let me re-index. The string is cols 10-49:
Row 49: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
Index:  0123456789...
Col 10 = B (index 0)
Col 11 = B (index 1)
...
Col 26 = B (index 16)
Col 27 = Y (index 17)
Col 28 = Y (index 18)
Col 29 = Y (index 19)
Col 30 = O (index 20)
Col 31 = O (index 21)
Col 32 = B (index 22)
...
Col 49 = B (index 39)
So at rows 49-53:
- Cols 10-26: B (black corridor, 17 cols)
- Cols 27-29: Y (yellow, 3 cols)
- Cols 30-31: O (orange, 2 cols)
- Cols 32-49: B (black corridor, 18 cols)
The left square is at cols 19-23, which is in the left black corridor. The right square is at cols 39-43, which is in the right black corridor.
If I press RIGHT:
- Left square moves from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28. But cols 27-29 are Y, so the square would overlap with yellow cells. Would this be allowed?
Actually, I observed earlier that the squares can only move on black corridors. So the left square can move from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28 only if cols 24-28 are all black. But cols 27-29 are yellow, so the square can't move there.
Wait, but I observed earlier that pressing RIGHT moved the left square from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28. Let me re-check...
Actually, I'm not sure. Let me look at the history more carefully. In my earlier exploration, I pressed RIGHT from the initial state and observed the squares moving. But I might have misremembered.
Let me just try pressing RIGHT from the current state and see what happens. The current state has:
- Left square: rows 49-53, cols 19-23
- Right square: rows 49-53, cols 44-48
Wait, the right square is at cols 44-48, not 39-43. This is because I moved it earlier. Let me first reset the squares to the initial position.
Actually, I can't reset. Let me just work with the current state.
Current state:
- Left square: rows 49-53, cols 19-23
- Right square: rows 49-53, cols 44-48
Let me try pressing RIGHT to see if the left square can move to cols 24-28.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current square positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
# Try RIGHT to see if left square can move right
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
